edges.description: HLA-restricted presentation activates drug-specific cytotoxic T cells.
Activated cytotoxic lymphocytes release mediators that kill keratinocytes.
Confluent keratinocyte death produces full-thickness epidermal necrosis.
Loss of the epidermal barrier produces systemic consequences.

nodes.description: Allopurinol/oxypurinol-specific CD8 T cells (and NK cells) are activated and expand, becoming the effector population that targets keratinocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis.
evidence.snippet: Drug-specific CD8 T-cells and natural killer cells are thought to be the major inducers of keratinocyte apoptosis via release of soluble cytotoxic mediators, including Fas ligand, perforin/granzyme, tumor necrosis factor, and granulysin.

nodes.description: Confluent keratinocyte death produces full-thickness epidermal necrosis that separates the epidermis from the dermis, manifesting as the skin and mucosal detachment of allopurinol SJS/TEN.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of the Pathogenesis of Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis.
evidence.snippet: Total epidermal necrosis separates the epidermis from the dermis.

nodes.description: Class I HLA allele present in essentially all allopurinol-SCAR patients in the index Han Chinese case-control study; the restricting element for drug-specific T-cell recognition.

meta.association: HLA-B*58:01 strongly predisposes to allopurinol-induced SCAR (SJS/TEN)

evidence.reference_title: HLA-B*5801 allele as a genetic marker for severe cutaneous adverse reactions caused by allopurinol.
evidence.snippet: The HLA-B*5801 allele was present in all (100%) 51 patients with allopurinol-SCAR, but only in 20 (15%) of 135 tolerant patients

evidence.reference_title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of HLA-B5801 genotyping in the treatment of gout patients with chronic renal insufficiency in Korea.
evidence.snippet: the HLA-B5801 allele and renal impairment are strongly associated with SCARs

nodes.description: Allopurinol and its active metabolite oxypurinol are presented in an HLA class I-restricted manner to drug-specific T cells; the HLA-B*58:01 allele provides the restricting element, making this the initiating immunological lesion of the conserved SCAR module in its allopurinol-specific form.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of the Pathogenesis of Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis.
evidence.snippet: acute TEN is considered a T-cell mediated, type IV hypersensitivity disorder. It mostly results from a cumulative effect of risks from the drug structure, drug metabolism, HLA alleles and T cell clonotypes.

evidence.reference_title: HLA-B*5801 allele as a genetic marker for severe cutaneous adverse reactions caused by allopurinol.
evidence.snippet: The HLA-B*5801 allele was present in all (100%) 51 patients with allopurinol-SCAR, but only in 20 (15%) of 135 tolerant patients

nodes.description: Activated lymphocytes release granulysin, Fas ligand, and perforin/granzyme, driving keratinocyte apoptosis and necroptosis — the central effector lesion of allopurinol SJS/TEN and the key conformance target of the SCAR module.

evidence.reference_title: Immunological response in Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis.
evidence.snippet: With regard to keratinocyte death, several cell death mediators, such as FasL, granulysin and annexin A1, have been proposed as playing a role in SJS/TEN pathogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of the Pathogenesis of Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis.
evidence.snippet: Apoptosis or necroptosis causes keratinocytes to lose their shape and adhesion, and necrosis predominates within a few days.

nodes.description: Extensive epidermal and mucosal detachment causes skin-barrier failure with high morbidity and mortality, the defining consequence of allopurinol SJS/TEN.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis.
evidence.snippet: immunologically mediated cutaneous adverse reaction characterized by mucous membrane and epidermal detachment, with a mortality ranging from 15% to 25%
